title: Lesson 3 Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome to lesson three, Luis. We'll cover personal pronouns, demonstratives — the words for 'this' and 'that' — and how to split Maltese words into syllables, which makes spelling easier.

Let's start with personal pronouns. These are the small words for I, you, he, she, we, and they. In Maltese, most of them have a long form and a short form. They mean the same thing — choose whichever feels natural.
These pronouns replace nouns. So instead of saying 'Jack is going to football', you can say
He goes to football. Instead of 'The children slept', you say
Notice the gender matches: a feminine noun becomes hi, a masculine noun becomes hu, and any plural becomes huma — that part is simpler than the noun system.
Now demonstratives — the words for this, that, these and those. In Maltese, these change with gender and number, just like adjectives.
For things close to you, use these three: dan for masculine — like
For things further away, the same three with a slight twist:
An easy way to remember: dan, din, dawn for what you can touch; dak, dik, dawk for what's over there.

Finally, syllables. Maltese is mostly phonetic — words are written close to how they sound — so splitting words into syllables is a powerful tool to help you spell. There are three rules. Each syllable has only one vowel. The second syllable starts with a consonant. And if two consonants sit next to each other in the middle of a word, you split between them.
and the longer word umbrella splits into
Three syllables. The interactive section will let you tap a card to reveal the split, with the audio played each time.
That's the third lesson. Tap into any section to drill what you've heard. Saħħa.
